subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 4. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to talk, show, compare and play alongside the child, using the simple scripts and activities set out here. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 1 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Compare the height of two objects and say which is taller and which is shorter.
Compare the length of two objects and say which is longer and which is shorter.
Compare the width of two objects and say which is wider and which is thinner.
Put three or four objects in order by height or by length.
Line objects up fairly at one end before comparing them.
Measure how tall or how long something is by counting blocks or paper clips.
Use full sentences such as ‘the date palm is taller than the bush’.
Notice that an object can be tall but thin, or short but wide — size has more than one direction.
A set of the same-size blocks or cubes for measuring height (wooden bricks, bottle caps stacked, or sugar cubes).

concepts.title: Comparing Height — Tall and Short
concepts.explanation: Start with height, because standing things up is easy to see. Height is how far it is from the bottom to the top. Stand two objects next to each other on the same flat surface — say a tall cup and a short cup — and talk about which reaches higher. How to teach it: Put the two cups side by side on the table so their bottoms rest on the same surface. Run your finger up the side of each one. Say, ‘This one is tall. This one is short. The big cup is taller than the small cup. The small cup is shorter than the big…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Look at the date palm and the little bush. Which one reaches higher?” Child: “The palm!” You: “Yes. The date palm is taller than the bush.” You: “So the bush is… shorter than the palm. Can…

concepts.title: Comparing Length — Long and Short
concepts.explanation: Length is how far it is from one end to the other, usually when a thing is lying down. A snake, a stick, a ribbon and a pencil all have length. The comparing words are longer and shorter. How to teach it: This is where fair lining-up matters most. To compare two sticks fairly, you must line them up so one end starts at the same place — for example, both against the edge of the mat. If one stick is pushed ahead of the other, the comparison is not fair. Show the child a ‘cheat’ where you slide a short stick forward…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us line up these two sticks. Both ends start here, at the edge of the mat.” You: “Now, which stick goes further? Which is longer?” Child: “This one is longer.” You: “Good. And the other…

concepts.title: Comparing Width — Wide and Thin
concepts.explanation: Width is how far across something is, from side to side. A road is wide; a little path is thin (we can also say narrow). A big anjero is wide; a pencil is thin. The comparing words are wider and thinner. How to teach it: Width is the trickiest of the three because the child must look across a thing rather than up it or along it. Use pairs where the difference is very clear at first: a wide ribbon and a thin ribbon, a fat marker and a thin pencil, a wide door and a thin gap. Sweep your finger from one side to the…
concepts.example: Bring the three ideas together with a simple sorting question: ‘Do we measure a tree by how tall it is, how long it is, or how wide it is?’ Talking about which word fits which object helps the child keep height, length…

concepts.title: Ordering by Size
concepts.explanation: Once the child can compare two things, move to three, then four. Putting objects in order means lining them up from shortest to tallest, or from shortest to longest. Start with just two, then add a middle one, then a fourth. How to teach it: Lay out three sticks of clearly different length. Ask, ‘Which is the shortest? Put it here first.’ Then, ‘Which of the rest is shorter? Put it next.’ Keep going until they are lined up. Then read the line together — ‘short, longer, longest’ — and try it the other way, from…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Here are three sticks. Find the shortest one and put it first.” You: “Now the next shortest. And now the longest goes at the end.” You: “Read them with me — short, longer, longest. You put…

concepts.title: Measuring with Blocks and Paper Clips
concepts.explanation: Now the child moves from comparing to measuring — finding out how many units long or tall something is. We use non-standard units: same-size blocks for height, paper clips for length. The child counts the units, which lovingly ties this unit back to counting from Unit 1. How to teach it: Stand a small toy next to a tower of blocks and count how many blocks reach its top — ‘the doll is 6 blocks tall’. To measure length, lay paper clips end to end beside a pencil and count them — ‘the pencil is 7 paper clips long’.…
concepts.example: A deeper idea to plant gently: the number depends on the size of the unit. The same pencil is more paper clips long than it is blocks long, because paper clips are smaller. You do not need the child to explain this —…

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Ordering by Size.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Once the child can compare two things, move to three, then four. Putting objects in order means lining them up from shortest to tallest, or from shortest to longest. Start with just two, then add a middle one, then a fourth. How to teach it: Lay out three sticks of clearly different length. Ask, ‘Which is the shortest? Put it here first.’ Then, ‘Which of the rest is shorter? Put it next.’ Keep going until they are lined up. Then read the line together — ‘short, longer, longest’ — and try it the other way, from…

To measure length, lay paper clips end to end beside a pencil and count them — ‘the pencil is 7 paper clips long’.…

questions.question: What does “Tall / taller than” mean?
options: How far from the bottom to the top, standing up
Not far from bottom to top; used for height and length
How far from one end to the other, lying down
How far across, from side to side
questions.answer: How far from the bottom to the top, standing up
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Tall / taller than means How far from the bottom to the top, standing up.

options: Not far from bottom to top; used for height and length
How far from one end to the other, lying down
How far across, from side to side

questions.explanation: Short / shorter than means Not far from bottom to top; used for height and length.

selfAssessment: I can compare the height of two objects and say which is taller and which is shorter.
I can compare the length of two objects and say which is longer and which is shorter.
I can compare the width of two objects and say which is wider and which is thinner.
I can put three or four objects in order by height or by length.
I can line objects up fairly at one end before comparing them.
I can measure how tall or how long something is by counting blocks or paper clips.
I can use full sentences such as ‘the date palm is taller than the bush’.
I can notice that an object can be tall but thin, or short but wide — size has more than one direction.
